metric_name: Hire date and role at Stratus
metric_value: Joined Stratus Computer on November 1, 1981 as Manager of Marketing Development, six weeks before product launch.
First product announced December 1981, six weeks after Kastner joined.
Hired to translate radical engineering into a market-moving story before product was public; 'I landed running.'
IBM 4300 would crash on component failure; recovery window of 30 minutes was unacceptable for ATM, airline reservations, stock trading.
source_page: The Problem That Created the Company

metric_value: Software-based fault tolerance using pairs of processors; programmers had to write explicit 'checkpointing' code for backup processor handoff.
source_page: The Problem That Created the Company

metric_value: Technically sound but expensive in the currency that mattered most: programmer time.
source_page: The Problem That Created the Company

metric_value: Founded in 1974 in Cupertino, CA.
source_page: The Problem That Created the Company

metric_value: Four physical microprocessors arranged in two pairs; hardware comparator checks results continuously; failed pair taken offline without losing a single clock cycle.
source_page: The Problem That Created the Company

metric_name: Hardware vs software fault tolerance distinction
metric_value: Stratus hardware fault tolerance hides failures from software entirely; application runs without awareness of failure event.
Calculate cost of downtime per hour, compare to price premium for fault-tolerant hardware, determine when the math works.
Prospects invited to pull a CPU board from a running system during transaction processing; system kept running, red LED lit on failed component.
'Ten seconds of theater replaced fifty pages of white papers.'
VP Hardware Gardner Hendrie could not reliably pull a board without crashing the system evening before public launch.
Shiny Lexan plastic covers on circuit boards generated static electricity when pulled; carpet doused with water solved launch-day problem; ECO #1 replaced Lexan with cardboard.
Engineering Change Order #1 replaced elegant Lexan plastic board covers with cardboard to eliminate static electricity risk.
Asked customers: 'What does one hour of downtime cost you?' — shifted IT managers from thinking about performance/capacity to quantifying availability.
ATM network processing 10,000 transactions/hour at $40 average value: straightforward arithmetic for cost-per-hour of downtime.
Created direct-mail pieces with cost-of-downtime calculators customized by industry; banking, brokerage, retail POS all covered.
Ran competitive displacement programs targeting Tandem users frustrated by NonStop programming complexity; message: 'Keep your programmers. Change your hardware.'
IBM came to Stratus after being nudged; IBM sales force watching enterprise customers evaluate fault-tolerant systems with nothing to offer them.
IBM agreed to rebrand the Stratus FT200 as the IBM System/88; Stratus marketing shifted to anchor: 'The Technology IBM Chose.'
'The Technology IBM Chose' neutralized every startup-risk objection; IBM brand eliminated uncertainty about Stratus viability.

metric_name: IBM OEM deal significance — IBM side
metric_value: IBM putting its name on a startup's product because it had no competitive alternative; validated Stratus without money buying such validation.
source_page: Sidebar: What the IBM OEM Deal Meant Strategically

metric_value: IBM salesforce carried System/88 competing with conventional IBM mainframes in some use cases; Kastner developed 'battlecard' materials explaining swim lanes.
source_page: Sidebar: What the IBM OEM Deal Meant Strategically

metric_value: Battlecards for IBM System/88 vs IBM 3090 were essentially conflict-resolution documents for a company simultaneously distributor and competitor.
source_page: Sidebar: What the IBM OEM Deal Meant Strategically

metric_value: By mid-1980s principal battlefield was banking/financial services; ATM networks and POS authorization systems expanding rapidly; Tandem and Stratus competed for every significant deal.
Tandem claimed Stratus hardware approach was brute-force redundancy; Stratus countered that Tandem's architectural elegance was paid for by every developer writing recovery code.
Turned hardware redundancy into a maintenance model: 'the board that mails itself' — pull failed board while system runs, mail it back, no on-site engineer.
'Zero-code advantage': on Stratus/System/88, standard COBOL or FORTRAN worked with fault tolerance as invisible infrastructure; no specialized NonStop programmers needed.
Total cost of ownership over five years including programmer time, training, ongoing maintenance often reversed hardware price advantage in Stratus's favor.
Made pivotal Stratus telecom market recommendation in a men's room; observed Rolm PBX systems needed exactly the reliability Stratus provided.
Rolm digital PBX switching millions of calls was an always-on application; telecom had highest cost of downtime of any industry.
Stratus became embedded infrastructure for SS7 signaling and emerging digital switching systems; five-nines uptime (99.999%, <6 min downtime/year) was entry requirement.
99.999% uptime — less than six minutes of downtime per year — was entry requirement for telecom contracts, not a sales argument.
Telecom DNA built in those years would define Stratus's long-term trajectory, leading to acquisition by Ascend and eventual integration into Nortel.
notes: Kastner writes this retrospectively from the vantage of post-1987 knowledge.

metric_value: Received cold call to present at Mitre Corporation in 1982; fifty silent engineers; declined to state requirements; Kastner had one hour.

metric_value: VP of Sales and Marketing at Stratus; ex-Honeywell; direct, likable, very funny; later became Cisco's first non-founder CEO.
John Morgridge later became Cisco's first non-founder CEO and built that company into a generation-defining enterprise.
John Morgridge arranged for daughter Kate to run trade shows for Kastner after Kastner spent 2.5 years running the program.
Turned out to be excellent — organized, energetic, genuinely good at the work.
At first show wore T-shirt reading 'Stratus Computers Never Go Down On You' to a union work-site; taught her about human nature in 1980s tech industry.
Kastner led market and product team for XA 2000 announcement before departing; machines built on Motorola 68020 processors.
XA 2000 delivered dramatic performance gains while preserving lockstep fault-tolerant architecture using Motorola 68020 processors.
John Logan (Yankee Group) retained Kastner to ghostwrite 100-page report 'The Future of Transaction Processing'; published January 1987.
Report predicted microprocessor-based OLTP price-performance was about to explode — Moore's Law meeting economics of departmental/distributed transaction processing.
Report predicted displacement of the mainframe was inevitable as microprocessor-based OLTP price-performance improved.
Stratus later quoted the Yankee Group OLTP study in its 1987 annual report; created mild conflict of interest for Kastner's next employer (DEC).
Former colleague from Prime Computer who had settled at Yankee Group; retained Kastner for OLTP ghostwriting.
Departed Stratus for Digital Equipment Corporation in 1987 after six years; next chapter (7) covers DEC.

metric_name: Lesson: superior engineering is necessary but not sufficient
metric_value: Stratus architecture was elegant but did not sell itself; demo, downtime calculator, IBM deal, and NORAD reference translated engineering advantage into market reality.

metric_name: Lesson: quantify the problem you solve
metric_value: 'Your ATM network loses $400,000 of transaction revenue per hour... our hardware costs $180,000 more' is an argument. 'We prevent downtime' is a claim.
ATM network loses $400,000 transaction revenue per hour of outage (implied benchmark used in sales arguments).
notes: Figure presented as hypothetical argument example rather than confirmed customer data.

metric_value: Fault-tolerant hardware costs $180,000 more than alternative (example figure in sales argument framing).
notes: Presented as example argument figure, not verified pricing data.

metric_name: Lesson: right market is not always obvious
metric_value: Launched into banking because downtime math was visible; telecom breakthrough came from sideways observation in men's room. Market planning staying in conference rooms misses oblique angles.
'Red lights sell more than white papers.' You could argue against a benchmark. You could not argue against a machine still running after you just pulled a CPU board.
Stratus eventually transitioned from proprietary Motorola chips to Intel architectures, maintaining lockstep edge as commodity hardware improved.
Tandem disappeared into HP (retrospective assessment).
notes: Tandem was actually acquired by Compaq in 1997, which was then acquired by HP in 2002.

metric_value: Survived as specialized platform for environments where hardware failure cannot wait for a technician: remote oil rigs, manufacturing floors, telecom nodes.
Vision articulated in the 1980s — computing infrastructure should be invisible, reliable, and self-healing — became baseline expectation for digital commerce.

metric_name: Stratus market position by exit year
metric_value: By 1987, technology had matured from regional curiosity to recognized platform in financial services, telecommunications, and mission-critical retail.
Kastner ran Stratus trade show program for approximately two and a half years before Kate Morgridge took it over.
ATM network processing 10,000 transactions an hour at $40 average value used as baseline in Stratus downtime cost marketing.
Most IT managers in 1981 thought about systems in terms of performance and capacity; availability was assumed; downtime was operational embarrassment, not financial metric.
In batch-processing world, 30-minute recovery was annoying but survivable; in ATM/airline/trading world, 30 minutes was catastrophe; 30 seconds was unacceptable.

metric_name: Most powerful marketing development at Stratus
metric_value: Most powerful marketing development of Kastner's Stratus years was not a campaign or brochure — it was the IBM OEM deal.
'No one ever got fired for buying IBM.' — Kastner invokes this heuristic to explain IBM OEM deal's risk-neutralization power.
What made the IBM/Stratus deal remarkable was the direction: IBM, the most dominant tech brand in the world, put its name on a startup's product because it had no competitive alternative.
source_page: Sidebar: What the IBM OEM Deal Meant Strategically

metric_value: Stratus architecture was arguably more elegant than Tandem's because it achieved same outcome with less burden on application developers.

metric_name: Downtime is not merely expensive — it is unacceptable
metric_value: In mission-critical computing, downtime is not merely expensive. It is unacceptable. That is what Stratus proved, one pulled circuit board at a time.
Six years at Stratus, 1981 to 1987.
